items.topic_thesis: Good myths describe how to be, not what is true.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Good myths describe how to be, not what is true.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking good myths describe how to be, not what is true.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Good myths describe how to be, not what is true.
publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Good myths describe how to be, not what is true.
publish_payload.hook: If your foundation story makes factual claims, it will die on the next frontier shift.
publish_payload.body_text:
If your foundation story makes factual claims, it will die on the next frontier shift.

A good myth does a different job. Knowledge explains the world and gets replaced when a better explanation arrives. Myth explains you. It tells you how to stand, how to act, what kind of person to be when the facts move under your feet.

That’s why the Cincinnatus story endures. You can’t disprove it by pointing to a leader who never returned to the plow. That person didn’t refute the myth. He failed to live inside it. The story isn’t a claim about what is true. It’s a claim about how to be.

This matters more now because expertise has a shorter half-life. When identity is tied to what you know, it dissolves with the knowledge. When identity is tied to a way of being, it can survive the update.

Choose myths that orient conduct, not myths that impersonate facts. One will steady people through change. The other will break with the first broken forecast.
publish_payload.footer_text: These posts are slices. The full program — the math, the rituals, the failure modes, the archetypes — is free at dionysusprogram.com.
publish_payload.final_text:
If your foundation story makes factual claims, it will die on the next frontier shift.

A good myth does a different job. Knowledge explains the world and gets replaced when a better explanation arrives. Myth explains you. It tells you how to stand, how to act, what kind of person to be when the facts move under your feet.

That’s why the Cincinnatus story endures. You can’t disprove it by pointing to a leader who never returned to the plow. That person didn’t refute the myth. He failed to live inside it. The story isn’t a claim about what is true. It’s a claim about how to be.

This matters more now because expertise has a shorter half-life. When identity is tied to what you know, it dissolves with the knowledge. When identity is tied to a way of being, it can survive the update.

Choose myths that orient conduct, not myths that impersonate facts. One will steady people through change. The other will break with the first broken forecast.

x_publish_payload.text:
Knowledge tells you what the world is. Myth tells you who to be when that answer changes.

That’s why Cincinnatus outlives better-informed eras. The story isn’t falsified by bad leaders. It survives as a demand, not a description.

items.topic_thesis: Lock-in is not trust; would they leave if they could is the real test.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Lock-in is not trust; would they leave if they could is the real test.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking lock-in is not trust; would they leave if they could is the real test.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Lock-in is not trust; would they leave if they could is the real test.
publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Lock-in is not trust; would they leave if they could is the real test.
publish_payload.hook: Some of the most committed-looking teams are just trapped.
publish_payload.body_text:
Some of the most committed-looking teams are just trapped.

The book draws a hard line between lock-in and trust. Lock-in is when people stay because leaving is costly: too much equity, too many burned bridges, too much identity tied up in the bet. Trust is when people with real options still choose the group.

The test is brutally simple: would they leave if they could?

If the answer is yes, you didn’t build trust. You built dependency. That can produce compliance for a while. It does not produce candor, resilience, or the kind of honesty you need when the ground is moving.

This matters now because more leaders are trying to force cohesion through intensity, implication, and sunk cost. Don’t confuse trapped people with bonded people. If you want truth under pressure, build the kind of place people are free to leave—and choose not to.
publish_payload.footer_text: I wrote The Dionysus Program because the tools I needed to lead through accelerating change did not exist in one place. Now they do, and they are free. dionysusprogram.com

Some teams don’t have trust. They have exit costs.

items.topic_thesis: The competitive threshold rises with cumulative environmental melt.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: The competitive threshold rises with cumulative environmental melt.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking the competitive threshold rises with cumulative environmental melt.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: The competitive threshold rises with cumulative environmental melt.
publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: The competitive threshold rises with cumulative environmental melt.
publish_payload.hook: The bar you have to clear is not standing still. It is rising while you debate.
publish_payload.body_text:
The bar you have to clear is not standing still. It is rising while you debate.

In the Epimetabolic Equation, that bar is the competitive threshold: the minimum explanatory reach required to remain viable. And it rises with cumulative environmental melt — the disruption imposed by the world through technology shifts, market changes, regulation, and competitor moves.

The important point is simple: ignored change still counts. Every new problem the environment creates becomes part of the standard. You do not get credit for refusing to engage it. The threshold moves up anyway.

That is why “wait and see” is usually misnamed. In a high-melt environment, standing still is not prudence. It is agreeing to be measured against a harder world with yesterday’s structure.
publish_payload.footer_text: The Dionysus Program is free at dionysusprogram.com. It was written for anyone whose head is out of the sand about what is coming next.
publish_payload.final_text:

x_publish_payload.text:
Change is not the risk. Unpriced change is.

You can reject a new tool, rule, or customer expectation. You cannot reject its effect on the threshold for staying viable. The bar rises first. Debate happens underneath it.

items.topic_thesis: Collaboration, mission or quality, and play are distinct channels for generating Ren.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Collaboration, mission or quality, and play are distinct channels for generating Ren.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking collaboration, mission or quality, and play are distinct channels for generating ren.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Collaboration, mission or quality, and play are distinct channels for generating Ren.
publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Collaboration, mission or quality, and play are distinct channels for generating Ren.
publish_payload.hook: When one person can do what ten used to do, trust stops being a byproduct of work.
publish_payload.body_text:
When one person can do what ten used to do, trust stops being a byproduct of work.

In the book, I call that missing current Ren: the density of trust, benevolence, and shared history between people. In the One-Man Job world, work no longer produces enough of it by accident.

Ren has three channels: collaboration on real shared problems, shared commitment to a mission—or, more honestly for most companies, to quality—and play, meaning unscored time together where status can suspend. A retreat cannot replace craft. Purpose-talk cannot replace contact. Mandatory fun is just work in costume.

That matters now because AI and distributed work are narrowing the first channel and faking the second. If you don’t build all three deliberately, you get Li without Ren: rituals, meetings, and culture language with no human current inside them.
publish_payload.footer_text: Knowledge explains the world. Myth explains you. The Dionysus Program is about maintaining both layers while everything accelerates. It is free at dionysusprogram.com.

x_publish_payload.text:
Retreats don’t replace shared craft.

Purpose talk can’t replace contact, and mandatory fun is just work in costume. As work gets more solo and more AI-assisted, teams gain ritual and lose the trust that used to come bundled with the job.

items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Oligarchic Decay.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking oligarchic decay.

publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: Oligarchic Decay.
publish_payload.hook: The scariest organizations are the ones that still have all the meetings.
publish_payload.body_text:
The scariest organizations are the ones that still have all the meetings.

That’s Oligarchic Decay: the rituals remain, but they’ve been captured by a self-perpetuating leadership class. The same people run the same forums for so long that every new idea gets filtered through one question: what will the founders think? The organization becomes a court, not a team.

In the book, this sits on Michels’s iron law of oligarchy. With too little rotation, stewardship decays. The forms are still there, but they stop being tools for collective sense-making and become performances for incumbents. Talented people see the ceiling and leave. Real correction comes late, usually from outside.

Why it matters now: when the world is changing fast, captured leadership doesn’t create stability. It creates lag disguised as continuity. If you won’t rotate stewardship, reality will do it for you.
publish_payload.footer_text: The Dionysus Program is a free book about raising epimetabolic rate — the speed at which a person, team, or organization converts error into new structure without consuming trust. dionysusprogram.com

A full calendar is not proof of institutional health. Sometimes it just means the court is still in session.

If every new idea has to survive an imagined conversation with the founders, the organization hasn’t built judgment. It has built deference.
